description: Reconciles three different first-letter absorption rates: 21.6% (iter_010), 27.1% (iter_009), 34.5% (iter_008)

rate_21_6.aggregation: per-token: FN/probe_correct_raw over all 11,725 token observations
rate_21_6.probe_training: 3,517 words * 4 prompts = 14,068 train tokens (separate from test)

rate_27_1.aggregation: per-token: FN/probe_correct_raw over 1,500 token observations (500 words * 3 prompts)

rate_34_5.aggregation: pilot run, likely small sample with different probe training
rate_34_5.note: This is from a PILOT run with smaller sample size. The 34.5% is a point estimate from limited data.

main_factors.details: iter_010 uses 2345 test words (full sae-spelling vocab), iter_009 uses 500 (subset). Larger sample dilutes rare-word absorption.
iter_010 uses 5 prompts/word, iter_009 uses 3 prompts/word. More prompt contexts may reduce context-dependent absorption.
iter_010 probe trained on 14,068 tokens (C=0.001), iter_009 on 4,132 tokens (C=0.01). Better-regularized probe may affect FN detection boundary.
Both use per-token aggregation (FN/probe_correct_raw). The difference is NOT an aggregation mismatch.
iter_008 value is from a pilot run with small sample. It was superseded by iter_009 FULL run (27.1%) using 500 test words.

aggregation_types.per_token: FN_count / probe_correct_raw_count over all token observations. Each word*prompt is one observation. This is the PRIMARY metric used in Table 2.
aggregation_types.per_word: For each unique word, compute word_absorption = FN_contexts / probe_correct_contexts, then average across words. This gives equal weight to each word regardless of sample count. Used for bootstrap CIs.
aggregation_types.per_letter: For each letter class, compute letter_absorption = FN / probe_correct in that class, then average across letters. Gives equal weight to each letter.

per_token_vs_per_word_gap.explanation: Per-word > per-token because low-frequency words tend to have higher absorption (the SAE lacks dedicated features for rare words), and per-word aggregation gives them equal weight.

rationale: 27.1% is from iter_009 FULL run, the authoritative experiment for the L24 16k cross-domain comparison
It matches the paper's Table 2 and is consistent across the paper narrative
21.6% comes from iter_010 which used a DIFFERENT probe (retrained with more data and different C), so it's a different experimental condition
21.6% is correctly cited as the 'probe degradation F1=1.0 control' but should NOT replace the 27.1% Table 2 value
34.5% is a superseded iter_008 pilot and should not appear in the final paper
The ~5.5pp gap between 21.6% and 27.1% is explained by larger test set (2345 vs 500 words) and better-regularized probe

paper_usage.Table_3_control: 21.6% (iter_010, the probe degradation experiment's undegraded baseline)
paper_usage.Note: These are from different experimental runs with different probes. The discrepancy is documented as 'control_check.delta' in probe_degradation.json
